parties: Böhm-Nordkartoffel Agrarproduktion-GmbH & Co. OHG v CPVO
title: CPVO BoA A021/2021 - Vineta
text:
Appeal Case A021/2021
DECISION
of the Board of Appeal of 14 December 2022
Concerning the appeal lodged by
Böhm-Nordkartoffel Agrarproduktion-GmbH & Co. OHG
Postfach 1380
D-21303 Lüneburg, Germany
Represented by Mrs. S. Ahrens von Bismarck, Rechtsanwältin
Rothenbaumchaussee 7
D-20148 Hamburg, Germany
Appellant
against a decision of
Community Plant Variety Office
3 boulevard Maréchal Foch
CS 10121,
49101 ANGERS CEDEX 2, FRANCE
Represented by Ms. O. Lamberti, Legal Advisor
Other Party to the Proceedings,
Relating to Community Plant Variety Right No EU 0527
Variety denomination: Vineta
Species: Solanum Tuberosum L.
On 14 December 2022, the Board of Appeal of the Community Plant Variety Office, composed of Mr. P. van der
Kooij (Chairman), Mr. K. Riechenberg (Rapporteur) and Mrs. C. Negro (Member), gave the following decision:
1. The decision of the Community Plant Variety Office of 1st October 2021 (with reference
NG/20210088416) labelled “Re: Corrigendum” is annulled.
2. The Office is ordered to bear the costs of the Appellant, pursuant to Article 85(1) of Council
Regulation (EC) No. 2100/94.

I. SUMMARY OF FACTS AND PROCEEDINGS
1. On 15 August 1995, the Community Plant Variety Office (hereinafter “CPVO” or “Office”) received an
application for a Community Plant Variety Right (hereinafter “CPVR”) for the variety Vineta of the species
Solanum Tuberosum L. (application No. 1995/1013). On 2 August 1996, by Decision No. EU 0527, the CPVO
granted the right for said variety for a period expiring at the latest on 1st August 2026.
2. At the time of the Community grant the variety Vineta enjoyed national protection in Germany as of 3 March
1994.
3. By letter of 1st October 2021 (reference NG/20210088416), under the heading “Re: Corrigendum”, the Office
informed the Appellant that it had identified the following “discrepancies” regarding the expiry date of
protection recorded for the variety Vineta in the sense that (1.) the reduction of the duration of a national
protection at the date of granting the CPVR from the 30 year duration had been “miscalculated” and that (2.)
the period of protection should run until the last day of the year in question, as specified by Article 19 of
Council Regulation (EC) No. 2100/94 (hereinafter “basic regulation”).
4. In the same letter the Office indicated to the Appellant that it had made the following “corrections”, pursuant
to Article 53(4) of Commission Regulation (EC) No. 874/2009 (hereinafter “implementing regulation”), to Title
No. EU 0527 concerning the variety denomination Vineta: Expiry date of protection: Original 01/09/2026 –
New: 31/12/2024. The CPVO added to its letter an Extract of the Register of 1st October 2021 containing the
modification of the expiry date of protection for the variety at issue and invited the Appellant to contact the
Office as to any queries regarding the matter.
5. By e-mail of 5 October 2021, the Appellant asked the CPVO to indicate the legal basis upon which this decision
had been taken. As understood by the Appellant, according to Article 19 of the basic regulation the duration
of protection for potato varieties amounts to 30 years beginning with the calendar year following the grant of
the CPVR, so that the protection given to the Vineta variety should end at 31 December 2027.
6. By letter of reply of 7 October 2021, the Office explained that, given the national protection awarded to the
variety in question as from 3 March 1994, a reduction of the Community protection granted of two full years
“should have been made” pursuant to Article 1(2) of Council Regulation (EC) No. 2470/96 (hereinafter “potato
regulation”). The CPVO stressed in the same letter that in the case of varieties for which a national plant
variety right was awarded prior to the grant of the CPVR, but to which Article 116(4), fourth indent, of the
basic regulation does not apply, the extension referred to in Article 1(1) of the potato regulation shall be
reduced by the longest period in full years during which any national property right granted has been effective
in a Member State in respect of the same variety prior to the grant of the CPVR.
7. On 14 October 2021, the CPVO received a notice of appeal against the communication of 1st October 2021.
8. By date of 28 January 2022, the Appellant filed a written statement setting out the grounds of appeal and
applied for an interlocutory revision of the decision contained in the letter of the Office of 1st October 2021.
The Appellant requested the CPVO to revoke said decision and to reinstate the prior status of the CPVR.
9. On 24 February 2022, the Rectification Committee of the Office proceeded to the requested interlocutory
revision of the appeal. It decided not to rectify the contested decision and to remit the appeal to the Board of
Appeal. The Committee also held that the appeal has suspensory effect.
10. By letter dated 31 March 2022, the Appellant submitted comments on the interlocutory revision declaring to
“repeat the appeal filed against the decision” requesting that the latter “be revoked” and the prior status of
the CPVR “be reinstated”. In its letter the Appellant reiterated the positions taken in the grounds of appeal
and formulated further considerations concerning the admissibility of the appeal.
2 / 8

11. On 8 June 2022 the Board of Appeal invited the CPVO to submit its views thereto by 7 August 2022. The Office
responded by requesting an extension of this time limit to 8 September 2022. Such extension was granted by
the Board.
12. The observations of the CPVO were duly received by this date.
13. In its brief the Office indicated, as a “preliminary remark”, that the comments of the Appellant of 31 March
2022 “should not be considered validly filed” and not be taken into consideration, because the latter had not
been invited by the Board to do so.
14. At the hearing which, on 30th November 2022, was conducted by videoconference both parties reiterated in
substance the arguments brought forward in their written submissions.
15. The Appellant requests the Board to declare the appeal admissible and to determine that the decision taken
by the CPVO on 1st October 2021 was incorrect and shall thus be withdrawn by the Office reinstating the prior
status and duration of the CPVR for the variety Vineta until 1st August 2026.
16. The CPVO asks the Board to reject the appeal and rule that the fees pertaining to it shall not be reimbursed
to the Appellant.
II. SUMMARY OF ARGUMENTS OF THE PARTIES
17. The Appellant claims that the appeal is admissible and well-founded arguing that the contested decision is null
and void, for formal (procedural) and substantive reasons. Therefore, the initial decision establishing the CPVR
in question remains valid with regard to the expiry of this right as well as the corresponding entry in the CPVO
register.
18. As far as formal (procedural) aspects are concerned the Appellant observes first that, contrary to Article 75 of
the basic regulation and Article 18 of the European Code of Good Administrative Behaviour (hereinafter “EU
code”), the contested decision did not state the grounds it is based upon nor a proper reasoning so as to
enable its addressee to comprehend the factual and legal elements behind it, i.e. the recalculation of the
duration of protection relating to the Vineta variety. Nor had the Office offered the opportunity to present
comments regarding said decision.
19. Second, the Appellant stresses that, contrary to Article 53(3) of the implementing regulation and Article 19 of
the CPVO code, the contested decision was not accompanied by a statement specifying possible means of
appeal as well as the time limits for lodging such an appeal.
20. Third, the Appellant points out that, contrary to Article 16 of the CPVO code, the Office did not provide for the
opportunity to submit written comments and present them before the decision was taken.
21. As far as the substantive aspects of the case are concerned, the Appellant takes the position that the
amendment to the entry in the CPVO register at issue is a decision subject to an appeal pursuant to Article 67
of the basic regulation.
22. The Appellant recalls that the decision to be corrected in the sense of Article 53(4) of the implementing
regulation is the decision adopted by the CPVO to grant a CPVR for the potato variety Vineta in 1996, which
was to last until 2026. This decision was, at the time it was taken, properly registered and documented in the
corresponding certificate. No mistake, no linguistic error, no error of transcription, nor any other obvious
inaccuracy in the transmission of the technical information can be identified when the variety in question was
entered into the CPVO register.
23. The Appellant claims that the Office did not incur in a “patent mistake” when the CPVR at issue was granted,
but that its decision was of substantial nature in the legal sense. The concept of “patent mistake” in Article
3 / 8

53(4) of the implementing regulation is limited to obvious formal mistakes, as opposed to an error in the
calculation of the expiry date of a CPVR. The amendment to the certificate operated by the Office affects the
scope and the substance of the decision granting the CPVR in question, which comprises the period of
protection relating thereto.
24. The Appellant indicates that there is no legal basis for a withdrawal (even partial) of a CPVR due to an
administrative error made by the competent authority. The only available options for a valid withdrawal of a
CPVR are laid down in Articles 20 and 21 of the basic regulation. However, none of the conditions for such a
decision applies in the present case.
25. The Appellant contests the view taken by the Office in its decision of 24 February 2022 that the correction in
the CPVO Register was only due to a “plain human oversight” and that it is to be qualified as a mere correction
of a “clerical mistake” within the meaning of Article 53(4) of the implementing regulation. On the contrary, a
modification of the duration of a CPVR constitutes a material change to the initial decision granting the CPVR.
26. The Appellant also invokes the principles of protection of legitimate expectations and legal certainty,
emphasising that by revoking its initial decision of 1996 and replacing it with a new one in 2021, thereby
reducing the duration of protection of the CPVR in question by a period of about two years, the CPVO has
disregarded the binding nature of a definitive decision adopted by it previously. The title holder was in no way
responsible for the wrong calculation of the duration of said right and has relied upon the CPVR grant at issue
in good faith for over 25 years.
27. The Appellant concludes that the decision taken in 1996 by which legal protection was granted to the variety
Vineta can neither be revoked retroactively nor altered with future effect on the ground of a mistake or
miscalculation in the past.
28. The position of the Office appears in points 13 to 29 of the decision taken on 24 February 2022 by the
Rectification Committee as well as in its submissions to the Board of Appeal of 8 September 2022.
29. The CPVO emphasises in the first place that the Appellant had not been invited by the Board to provide
comments regarding the interlocutory revision adopted on 24 February 2022. It follows from a combined
reading of Articles 71(2) and 76 of the basic regulation that it is incumbent upon the Board to ask the parties
to furnish observations relating to the dispute pending before it and that, in the absence of a request thereto,
any brief filed should be disregarded.
30. According to the CPVO the appeal is not admissible, since the calculation of the CPVR duration, under the non-
transitional period, for the variety at issue is not a matter of legal interpretation, but an operation correcting a
“plain human oversight”. Such a “corrigendum of duration” adopted to rectify a “clerical mistake” cannot be
subject to an appeal according to Article 67 of the basic regulation which, due to the wording “shall lie”,
contains an exhaustive list of the decisions that can be subject to an appeal. While said provision refers to
decisions concerning entries to and cancellations in the CPVO register, it does not embrace amendments of
entries which may be realized in situations of “patent mistakes” according to Article 53(4) of the implementing
regulation.
31. Emphasising its position on the admissibility of the appeal, the Office explains that, since the variety at stake
was of the species Solanum Tuberosum L., its duration of protection was extended by five years pursuant to
the potato regulation which entered into force after the grant of the CPVR. However, said regulation also
provides for a reduction of this duration corresponding to the national protection already enjoyed by the variety
to be calculated in full years, resulting in the setting of the date of expiration on 31 December 2024. The
mistake made regarding said date should be qualified as not only “obvious and apparent”, but also as of a
“formal and not substantial nature” in the sense that it concerned a “numerical calculation” and not an
“assessment of substantial nature” affecting or reducing the scope of the right granted.
32. As regards the obligation to state grounds as required by Article 75 of the basic regulation, the CPVO considers
to have provided “concise and precise explanations of the calculations performed”.
4 / 8

33. The Office takes the view that, according to Article 53(3) of the implementing regulation, it is required to
indicate in any decision that an appeal is possible. However, this is the case only for an appealable decision
referred to in Article 67 of the basic regulation. In the present case no such decision has been adopted.
34. The CPVO underlines that the Appellant has not been deprived of the right to formulate comments. In fact
such right has been exercised.
35. As to the application of the transitional regime established by Article 116 of the basic regulation, the Office
argues that in the balance between the concept of legal certainty on the one hand and the principle of legality
of administrative decisions on the other the latter should prevail. The “legitimate interest” and the “individual
expectations” of the title holder cannot override the “public interest” in the maintenance of an “accurate and
reliable” register of exclusive rights.
36. The Office concludes therefore that the principle of legal certainty cannot justify the “reiteration of a mistake
committed in the past”. On the contrary, the CPVO must have the legal faculty to “open closed proceedings”
in order to rectify an incorrect element concerning the duration and expiration of a CPVR.
III. REASONS
On the objection raised by the Office against the submissions brought forward by the Appellant dated 31 March
2022
37. The Board of Appeal notes that while, according to Article 71(2) of the basic regulation, when examining the
appeal, the Board “shall as often as necessary invite the parties … to file observations on notifications issued
by itself or on communications from the other parties to the appeal proceedings …”, the unsolicited document
presented by the Appellant dated 31st March 2022 cannot be disregarded.
38. In fact, the Board has accepted the submissions of the Appellant by inviting the CPVO to comment upon them.
In exercising its discretion and in compliance with the principle of procedural economy, the Board considered
it unnecessary to invite the Appellant to file observations on the interlocutory revision adopted on 24 February
2022, given that such observations had already been spontaneously submitted by the Appellant. It would be
indeed inconsistent with a proper conduct of the proceedings before the Board to deprive the Appellant of the
opportunity to react in detail to the decision of the Rectification Committee of the Office and the numerous
legal developments contained therein.
39. The submissions presented by the Appellant dated 31st March 2022 are therefore held to be taken into account.
On the admissibility of the appeal
40. In view of the arguments brought forward by the Office the Board has to determine in the first place whether
the action introduced by the Appellant concerns a decision against which an appeal is available.
41. In the present case the communication of the CPVO dated 1st October 2021 and labelled “Re: Corrigendum”,
read together with the explanations given thereto by letter dated 7th October 2021, is to be regarded as the
administrative measure against which the appeal is directed.
42. The Office arguing that this communication cannot be subject to an appeal, it is necessary to examine the
legal nature of said document.
43. More precisely, the Board is called upon to evaluate whether the communication at issue is governed by Article
53(4) of the implementing regulation, stipulating that “linguistic errors, errors of transcription and patent
mistakes in decisions of the Office shall be corrected”. If this were the case, said communication could not be
5 / 8

qualified as an appealable decision. On the other hand, should Article 53(4) of this regulation not be applicable,
the Board has to determine whether the administrative act in question is nonetheless a decision subject to an
appeal.
44. Both parties have put forward divergent interpretations of this provision.
45. The Board recalls in this respect that the General Court in its judgment of 25 June 2020 (Siberia Oriental/CPVO,
case T-737/18, point 52; hereinafter “Siberia judgment”) has ruled that the “only legitimate purpose” of
corrections made on the basis of said provision is to correct “spelling mistakes or grammatical errors, errors
of transcription … or errors which are so obvious that nothing but the wording as corrected could have been
intended”. In addition, in point 53 of the same judgment, the General Court has underlined that the rule
allowing for such corrections “is to be interpreted strictly”.
46. Hence, a “mistake” or “error” in the sense of Article 53(4) of the implementing regulation must be of such
characteristics that it can be identified by a simple reading of the body of the decision itself. No such “mistake”
or “error” can be established on the basis of this provision when it is necessary to have recourse to statutory
interpretations of EU and national law as well as calculations of time limits relating to the specifics of the variety
concerned in order to identify an erroneous legal determination in the decision at issue.
47. The Board observes that the amendment to the Register operated by the CPVO relates to the scope and
substance of the CPVR validly granted in 1996 by reducing the duration of said right. Due to its material
consequence the measure taken by the Office must be qualified as a substantive decision which affects the
timely dimension of the right in question, the length of protection being an essential integral part of the latter.
This legal qualification cannot be circumvented by its labelling as a “corrigendum”. Also, allowing the CPVO to
amend the scope and substance of a CPVR by way of recourse to Article 53(4) of the implementing regulation
would deprive the holder of it of the faculty to have legal redress against such measure.
48. It follows that the above-mentioned provision is not applicable in the present case.
49. The CPVO argues nevertheless that the communication of 1st October 2021 is not covered by Article 67 of the
basic regulation and is therefore not amenable to an appeal.
50. The Board holds that this position cannot be sustained.
51. Indeed, Article 67 of the basic regulation, as indicated in its first paragraph, must be read in conjunction with
its Article 87 as far as “the entering or deletion of information in the Register” is concerned (Siberia judgment,
point 36). In this regard, Article 87(2), letter e, stipulates that the CPVO Register shall comprise “the date on
which the Community plant variety right begins and ends, together with the reasons for the termination of
right”. The amendment of the expiry date of the CPVR amounts to “entering of information in the Register” as
regards “the date on which the Community plant variety right ends” in accordance with Articles 67 and 87(2),
letter e of the basic regulation.
52. As outlined in paragraph 47 of the present decision, the administrative act under examination has produced
legal effects upon the CPVR validly granted and held by the Appellant.
53. The Board thus considers that, on proper interpretation of Articles 67 and 87 of the basic regulation, the
measure adopted by the Office constitutes a decision against which an appeal must be allowed. Otherwise,
the Appellant would be deprived of a legal remedy against a decision concerning the CPVR granted to its
holder.
54. Indeed, the principle of effective judicial review mandates that no administrative act adopted in the field of
application of EU law having legal effects can escape review by the competent judicial or quasi-judicial
authority. The European Court of Justice has underlined the overriding importance of said principle on multiple
occasions, in particular in its judgments of 23 April 1986 (Les Verts/European Parliament, case 294/83, ECR
p.1339, points 23 to 25), of 15 May 1986 (Johnston, case 222/84, ECR p.1651, point 18), of 15 October 1987
6 / 8

(Heylens, case 222/86, ECR p.4112, point 14) and of 3 December 1992 (Borelli/Commission, case C-97/91,
ECR p.I-6313, point 14).
55. It follows from this jurisprudence that in the present dispute an appeal must be allowed, the contested
amendment to the Register having produced precise detrimental effects upon the legal position held by the
Appellant.
56. The appeal, having been introduced in due form and time, is therefore admissible.
On the substance of the appeal
57. It is appropriate to examine first the substantive arguments put forward by the Appellant (paragraphs 21 to
27 of the present decision).
58. The Board observes that there is no provision in the basic regulation allowing the Office to alter the content
of a CPVR validly granted, such as the length of protection contained therein.
59. Given this absence of a statutory basis for such a substantive modification of the right concerned, the Office
must establish that a general principle of law enables it to adopt a measure reducing the duration of a CPVR.
60. In this regard the CPVO has argued that the “public interest” in the existence of a correct Register constitutes
such a general principle of law and a legal basis to withdraw in part the protection granted by the initial
decision.
61. The Board emphasises that, supposing such “public interest” is to be recognised, said principle must be
balanced against other general principles of law the Appellant may rely upon, such as the concept of legal
certainty, the protection of a validly acquired CPVR by way of an administrative decision having become final
as well as the recognition of legitimate expectations of the title holder generated by such a decision, such as
those based upon the duration of the right in question.
62. It also must be underlined that the erroneous calculation of the duration of the CPVR validly awarded occurred
more than twenty years ago without having been detected by the competent administrative unit of the CPVO
and could in no way be attributed to misinformation or wrongful statements made by the Appellant in the
context of the application for protection of the variety at issue.
63. The Board recalls that legal certainty is one of a number of general principles recognised by EU law (Siberia
judgment, point 34).
64. The Board finds that the contested amendment to the Register has the effect of undermining the binding
nature of the definitive decision granting the CPVR in question in the sense of the considerations contained in
point 53 of this judgment of the General Court. This measure must therefore be regarded as being incompatible
with the principle of legal certainty.
65. In view of these particular circumstances which characterize the present dispute the Board is of the opinion
that the Office has failed to demonstrate that the alleged “public interest” should prevail over the general legal
principles relied upon by the Appellant as indicated in paragraph 61 of the present decision.
66. Given this appreciation it is not necessary to evaluate the arguments raised by the Appellant relating to
procedural (formal) aspects of the proceedings before the Office (paragraphs 18 to 20 of the present decision).
67. It follows that the decision adopted by the CPVO on 1st October 2021, labelled “Re: Corrigendum” is invalid.
7 / 8

IV. CONCLUSION
68. Said decision must therefore be annulled.
V. ON THE COSTS
69. According to Article 85(1) of the basic regulation, the losing party to appeal proceedings shall bear the costs
incurred by the other party to the proceedings. The Appellant having prevailed in its submissions, the Office
is ordered to bear said costs.
VI. ON LEGAL REMEDIES
70. Pursuant to Article 73 of the basic regulation an action may be brought before the Court of Justice against this
decision within two months of its service.
Paul VAN DER KOOIJ Kurt RIECHENBERG
Chairman of the Board of Appeal Rapporteur
8 / 8
